title: The Death of Nature: Women, Ecology, and the Scientific Revolution
excerpt: A groundbreaking feminist analysis of how the Scientific Revolution replaced an organic, feminine view of nature with a mechanistic, exploitative one.
keyIdeas: Pre-modern Western culture viewed nature as a living, nurturing mother - an organic worldview
The Scientific Revolution of the 16th-17th centuries replaced this with a mechanistic, dead view of nature
The shift to mechanism was intertwined with the subordination of women
The 'death of nature' licensed unrestrained exploitation of both land and women
keyTakeaways: The metaphor of nature as female made the exploitation of nature seem natural and justified
Francis Bacon's scientific program used explicitly gendered language of domination and control
The witch hunts and the rise of mechanism were historically concurrent and ideologically linked
Recovering an organic worldview is essential for ecological survival
whoShouldRead: Anyone interested in the cultural roots of the ecological crisis
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a straightforward ecological or natural history book
Those allergic to feminist analysis or postmodern theory
